**Leonilde** "**Nilde**" **Iotti** (Italian pronunciation: [[(leo)ˈnilde ˈjɔtti]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); 10 April 1920 – 4 December 1999) was an Italian politician, member of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI). Towards the end of World War Two, she fought as a partisan for the Italian resistance. She was the first and only woman member of the PCI to become the president of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "Chamber of Deputies (Italy)"), an office she held for three consecutive legislatures from 1979 to 1992, becoming the longest-serving post-war president of the Chamber.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nilde_Iotti&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Leonilde Iotti was born in [Reggio Emilia](/wiki/Reggio_Emilia "Reggio Emilia") in 1920. Her father Egidio was a [railroader](/wiki/Railroader "Railroader") and a socialist [trade unionist](/wiki/Trade_unionist "Trade unionist"), fired by the [railway](/wiki/Railway "Railway") company on account of his political commitment. Her father died in 1934, but, thanks to a [scholarship](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship"), she attended the [Catholic University of Milan](/wiki/Universit%C3%A0_Cattolica_del_Sacro_Cuore "Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore"), graduating in literature in 1942. Amongst her professors at the university was [Amintore Fanfani](/wiki/Amintore_Fanfani "Amintore Fanfani"), the future Christian Democratic leader and [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy").[[1]](#cite_note-1) On 5 October 1942, Iotti became a member of the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party") (PNF) within the Federation of Female Fascists of Reggio Emilia. Membership was required in order to become a teacher.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

After [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini")'s downfall in July 1943 and [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio")'s [proclamation](/wiki/Badoglio_Proclamation "Badoglio Proclamation") on September 8, which caused the beginning of the [civil war](/wiki/Italian_civil_war "Italian civil war"), Iotti became interested in communist ideals and took part in the [resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") against the [Nazi German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") invaders during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Political career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nilde_Iotti&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Leonilde_Iotti.jpg/250px-Leonilde_Iotti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonilde_Iotti.jpg)

Official portrait of Iotti from 1948

After the end of the war and the [referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") against the [Savoy Monarchy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), in 1946, Iotti was [elected](/wiki/1946_Italian_general_election "1946 Italian general election") member of the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_Italy "Constituent Assembly of Italy"), within the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI). She was also one of the 75 members of the Committee entrusted with the drafting of the [Italian Republican Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy").

In April 1948, Iotti was re-elected with the PCI to the Chamber of Deputies, of which she was a member without interruption until 1999. In 1956, she became a member of the Central Committee of the Party and in 1962 of the National Direction. Re-elected to the Chamber in [1963](/wiki/1963_Italian_general_election "1963 Italian general election"), she was appointed to the Constitutional Affairs Commission, focusing her activity on the relevance of the female role in the world of work and family relationships. In the following years, her main commitment became the reform of [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights"), notably the right to [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"). She was particularly involved in the campaign for the [1974 divorce referendum](/wiki/1974_Italian_divorce_referendum "1974 Italian divorce referendum").

After the [1979 Italian general election](/wiki/1979_Italian_general_election "1979 Italian general election"), thanks to the support of PCI's historic rival, [Christian Democracy](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)") (DC), Iotti became [President of the Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/President_of_the_Chamber_of_Deputies_(Italy) "President of the Chamber of Deputies (Italy)"), with 443 votes out of 615. She succeeded another communist, [Pietro Ingrao](/wiki/Pietro_Ingrao "Pietro Ingrao"). Iotti focused her first speech in front of the House on women's role in society as well as the fight against terrorism.

Iotti was popular and respected as a president, and was confirmed in the office for two more legislatures. In 1987, she was entrusted by [President](/wiki/President_of_Italy "President of Italy") [Francesco Cossiga](/wiki/Francesco_Cossiga "Francesco Cossiga") with a mandate of potentially forming a government. She became the first communist and the first woman to have ever received an exploratory mandate to become [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy"); however, Iotti was not able to form a coalition.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 1992, Iotti's name was put forward for the election for [President of the Italian Republic](/wiki/President_of_the_Italian_Republic "President of the Italian Republic").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nilde_Iotti&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Leonilde was an atheist.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1946, she began her relationship with the communist leader [Palmiro Togliatti](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti "Palmiro Togliatti"), who was 27 years older than her. This lasted until his death in 1964. Their relationship, which was kept secret in the early years, became public knowledge in 1948 after an [attempt on Togliatti's life](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti#Salerno_Turn_and_assassination_attempt "Palmiro Togliatti") a few days after the [general election](/wiki/1948_Italian_general_election "1948 Italian general election"). Their relationship was opposed by Italy's public opinion, including many communists, because Togliatti was already married to [Rita Montagnana](/wiki/Rita_Montagnana "Rita Montagnana") at the time. However, Togliatti forced his wife Rita and their son Aldo, who was mentally ill, to live in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), so he could continue his relationship with Iotti. Together they asked for and obtained the adoption of an orphan girl, Marisa Malagoli. Marisa was the younger sister of one of the six workers killed by [Carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri") on 9 January 1950 in [Modena](/wiki/Modena "Modena"), during a workers' demonstration.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nilde_Iotti&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death")]

Iotti died in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") on 4 December 1999, and is buried in the [Cimitero del Verano](/wiki/Campo_Verano "Campo Verano").
